name: Briareus
description: Briareus, also known as Aegaeon, was a Hecatoncheire, one of the hundred-handed giants in Greek mythology. Born from Gaia (Earth) and Uranus (Sky), Briareus and his brothers Cottus and Gyges possessed immense strength and a hundred arms, each wielding fifty heads. Their terrifying appearance and immense power led Uranus to imprison them within the Earth. They were later freed by Zeus during the Titanomachy, the great war between the Olympian gods and the Titans. Briareus and his brothers proved instrumental in securing Zeus's victory, hurling massive rocks at the Titans, overwhelming them with their combined might. After the war, Briareus became a guardian of Tartarus, the abyss where the Titans were imprisoned. Some accounts also credit him with helping Poseidon and Zeus when they were bound by Hera, Athena, and Apollo, using his hundred hands to free the king of the gods. He is a symbol of overwhelming force and unwavering loyalty.
subtitle: Hundred-Handed Giant of Immense Strength
appearance: Briareus is depicted as a colossal giant with a hundred arms and fifty heads. His appearance is terrifying and awe-inspiring, reflecting his immense power. He is typically shown as muscular and imposing, a symbol of raw strength.
habitat: Initially imprisoned within the Earth, Briareus later resided near the sea and served as a guardian of Tartarus.
behavior: Briareus is characterized by his unwavering loyalty and immense strength. He is a powerful ally and a fearsome opponent. He is depicted as being helpful to the Olympian gods, especially Zeus, and is a staunch defender of their rule.

keyMyths.title: Birth and Imprisonment
keyMyths.description: Born from Gaia and Uranus, Briareus and his brothers were imprisoned by Uranus due to their terrifying appearance and power, fearing they would overthrow him. This highlights the fear of the unknown and the potential for children to surpass their parents in power.

keyMyths.title: The Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: Freed by Zeus, Briareus and his brothers aided the Olympians in their war against the Titans, using their hundred hands to hurl massive rocks and overwhelm the Titans. Their contribution was crucial to the Olympian victory, demonstrating the importance of unlikely allies.

keyMyths.title: Guardian of Tartarus
keyMyths.description: After the Titanomachy, Briareus was appointed as a guardian of Tartarus, ensuring the Titans remained imprisoned. This role signifies his strength and trustworthiness, making him a reliable warden of the underworld's deepest prison.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides an account of the birth of Briareus and his brothers.
sources.text: Provides an account of the birth of Briareus and his brothers.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Mentions Briareus's role in assisting Zeus.
sources.text: Mentions Briareus's role in assisting Zeus.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Describes Briareus's involvement in the Titanomachy.
